children.name: Proteus anguinus
children.description: Blind, pale, neotenic cave dweller of Dinaric karst in the Balkans; navigates by electroreception and chemoreception; can survive 10 years without food; documented individuals over 100 years old; IUCN Vulnerable; the 'human fish' of Slovenian cave legend.

children.name: Necturus maculosus
children.description: Permanently aquatic; retains large bushy external gills throughout adult life; bottom-dweller of eastern lakes and rivers; named from the false belief it could bark like a dog.

children.name: Necturus alabamensis
children.description: A permanently aquatic salamander restricted to rocky-bottomed streams and rivers of the Coosa River drainage in Alabama. Like all waterdogs it retains external gills throughout life and is active mainly at night, hunting crayfish, small fish, and aquatic invertebrates under submerged rocks and leaf litter. Its restricted range and sensitivity to siltation and water quality degradation make it vulnerable to threats from agriculture and development. Listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN and protected under Alabama state law.

children.name: Necturus lewisi
children.description: Endemic to the Neuse and Tar river basins in North Carolina, this waterdog inhabits rocky and muddy river bottoms with abundant leaf litter and woody debris. It is distinguished from other waterdogs by its dark, spotted pattern on a brownish background. A nocturnal predator of invertebrates and small fish, it is threatened by sedimentation, pesticide runoff, and water quality degradation from urban and agricultural land use. Named after Joseph Bailey Lewis, a naturalist and physician who collected natural history specimens in North Carolina.

children.name: Necturus lodingi
children.description: One of the rarest and most poorly known salamanders in North America, confined to the Black Warrior River system in north-central Alabama. It favours large rivers and their tributaries with rocky substrates and good water quality, spending most of its life hidden under large flat rocks. Its entirely dark, unpatterned colouration distinguishes it from other Alabama waterdogs. Critically endangered due to its extremely restricted range and ongoing habitat degradation from coal mining, agriculture, and urban runoff in the Black Warrior watershed. Named after Henry P. Loding, a naturalist and entomologist from Mobile, Alabama.
